elements.text: The first pale light of daybreak cuts through the jagged teeth of the Scarred Gorge. Mist rises from the abyss, not damp and cold, but thick with the oily smell of burnt marrow and ancient, decaying metal.
Kaelen stands near the precipice, his eyes narrowed as he traces the geometric fractures in the stone below. He draws a map of lines in his mind, calculating angles of approach.
The wind is funneling southward. If we draw it toward the narrowest ridge, we can limit its sweep to fifteen degrees. We strike when its mass shifts.
Lyra's hand rests on the pommel of her blade, her knuckles white. She stares down into the roiling dark of the gorge, her chest rising and falling in shallow, uneven breaths.
You talk of angles as if we are fighting a siege engine, Kaelen. The air itself... it feels wrong. It feels hungry.
Master Valerius steps forward, his heavy boots grinding grit into the stone. He raises his chin, his weathered face catching the gray morning light.
She has the right of it. The mountain is not breathing. Something else is using its lungs.

A sudden, sickening pressure drops over the gorge. It is not a physical blow, but a crushing weight of pure, malicious intent that forces their knees to buckle. The shadows at the bottom of the crevasse peel away from the rocks, rising like a towering wall of liquid blackness that blots out the sunrise.
The air hums with a thousand whispering voices, crawling into their ears, scratching at their thoughts. Lyra stumbles back, pressing a hand to her temple, her eyes wide with uncharacteristic dread.
This... this is not a beast. It's a rot. It knows exactly what we are.

Kaelen tries to step forward, but the absolute gravity of the manifestation freezes his muscles. His analytical mind, usually so swift to categorize and dismantle, spins in place, finding zero paths to victory.
The energy output... it bypasses standard cultivation thresholds. There is no structural weakness. Any direct assault has a zero-percent probability of survival.
Valerius looks at Kaelen, then down at the rising tide of shadow. The sorrow in the old master's eyes deepens into a cold, hard certainty.
It is exactly what the histories warned of. It does not want to fight us, Kaelen. It wants to hollow us out.
The shadow wall surges, a wave of absolute cold creeping up the stone path toward them. Lyra draws her sword, but her stance is fractured, her blade trembling.
How do we fight something that is already inside our heads? Tell me, Kaelen! What is the plan?
Kaelen stares at his own hands, realizing the sheer, horrific scale of the entity. For the first time, his calculations offer no solution, only retreat.
We don't. We fall back. Right now.

Valerius does not retreat. He steps between the two young cultivators and the encroaching dark, his shoulders squaring as he begins to draw upon his own life essence, a faint, golden aura flickering against the overwhelming black.
You cannot outrun this, not both of you. Go, Architect. Find another way. I will pay the toll to buy you the minutes.
